coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from roasted mackerel (scomberomorus niphonius) for free radical scavenging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiaqi
Cao, Lin
Li, Dongmei
Yu, Chenxu
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Foodborne nanoparticles produced from food components during thermal processing have received attention due to their particular properties. Herein, the presence of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was reported from mackerel flesh roasted at 230 °C in an electronic oven, and the CD capability for free radical scavenging was investigated. The average size of the CDs was about 2.2 nm with a size distribution in the range of 0.9–3.5 nm as characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed that the mackerel CDs mainly contain C, N and O. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis showed the existence of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the mackerel CD surface. The fluorescence lifetime was about 9.1 ns. Two absorption peaks at 277 and 328 nm were observed in UV–vis spectra, which are assigned to the π-π* and n-π* charge-transfer, respectively. The mackerel CDs showed excellent photostability in various metal ion solutions and pH environment. In addition, these CDs exhibited strong dose-dependent scavenging capability for hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and methyl radicals (·CH3) produced from methylene blue (MB)/visible-light photosensitization system. The free radical scavenging properties make the mackerel CDs capable of protecting against oxidative stress after they are exposed to biosystem.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Foodborne nanoparticles
Free radical
Fluorescence
Roasted mackerel
Fluorescent carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of CDs from the roasting mackerel
Effects of mackerel CD on hydroxyl radicals
Effects of mackerel CD on MB degradation
Characterization of fluorescent CDs in roasted mackerel
Fluorescence and its mechanism of CDs
Effects of mackerel CD on hydroxyl radicals
Effects of mackerel CDs on methyl radical scavenging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the CDs produced from roasted mackerel. Photographs of (b) raw and (c) roasted mackerel, and corresponding extractant aqueous solution under the irradiation of ultra-violet light from (b) raw and (c) roasted mackerel. (d) TEM image of CDs from roasted mackerel. Insets show corresponding particle size histogram of the CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of mackerel CDs for various excitation wavelengths. (b) Normalized fluorescence spectra to show the red-shifting with the increase of excitation wavelength of Fig. 2a. (c) Fluorescence decay curve and (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of mackerel CDs. Inset shows the magnification of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of mackerel CDs. FL: fluorescence, a. u.: arbitrary unit (the same below). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra (a), XPS spectrum (b), high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s and (d) N1s of the mackerel CDs. Fish powder used here were obtained from the mackerel flesh after freeze-drying.

Effects of (a) photostability, (b) metal ions, (c) concentration of NaCl and (d) pH value on the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the CDs aqueous solution excited at 380 nm. Single asterisk (*) means difference with a p value < 0.05, and double asterisks (**) represent significant difference with a pvalue < 0.01.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of H2O2 and hydroxyl radicals from Fenton reaction. (c) ESR spectra of the Fenton solution treated with different amount of CDs. (d) The inhibition of hydroxyl radicals by the mackerel CDs was calculated as I=(1-I/I0)%.
ESR spectra of MB at different irradiation time in the absence (a) and presence (b) of CDs, CDs/DMPO-only (c) and DMPO-only (d) solution.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB in the absence and presence of CDs with residual ratio (C/C0) of MB under dark condition; (b) Photocatalytic degradation of MB in the absence and presence of CDs with residual ratio (C/C0) of MB under visible light irradiation; (c) Photocatalytic reaction kinetics of MB degradation in the presence of CDs; (d) Fluorescence spectra changes of MB-containing CD aqueous solution at different irradiation time. The fluorescence spectrum of MB was included for comparison.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots from roasted mackerel (scomberomorus niphonius) for free radical scavenging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian, 116034, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian, 116034, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agricultural and Biosystems Engineering, Iowa State University, Ames, IA, 50010, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Agricultural and Biosystems Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Agricultural and Biosystems Engineering, Iowa State University, Ames, IA, 50010, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Qinggongyuan1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian, 116034, Liaoning, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Foodborne nanoparticles produced from food components during thermal processing have received attention due to their particular properties. Herein, the presence of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was reported from mackerel flesh roasted at 230 °C in an electronic oven, and the CD capability for free radical scavenging was investigated. The average size of the CDs was about 2.2 nm with a size distribution in the range of 0.9–3.5 nm as characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed that the mackerel CDs mainly contain C, N and O. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis showed the existence of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the mackerel CD surface. The fluorescence lifetime was about 9.1 ns. Two absorption peaks at 277 and 328 nm were observed in UV–vis spectra, which are assigned to the π-π* and n-π* charge-transfer, respectively. The mackerel CDs showed excellent photostability in various metal ion solutions and pH environment. In addition, these CDs exhibited strong dose-dependent scavenging capability for hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and methyl radicals (·CH3) produced from methylene blue (MB)/visible-light photosensitization system. The free radical scavenging properties make the mackerel CDs capable of protecting against oxidative stress after they are exposed to biosystem.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have drawn great attention over the past decades due to their extremely small feature size, high surface to volume ratio and promising physicochemical properties (Becker et al., 2014; Nie et al., 2015; Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012; Zhang & Chen, 2014). When the nanomaterials are applied in food industry, nowadays in a perspective of an integrated food research approach, three aspects including the quality, safety and potential nutraceutical value of a food are concerned (Daliu, Santini, & Novellino, 2018; Santini et al., 2018; Santini, Novellino, Armini, & Ritieni, 2013). Many modern physicochemical methods like grinding, hydrothermal reaction, microwave assisted synthesis, and combustion thermal oxidation, have been used for the synthesis of nanoparticles (Deguchi, Mukai, Tsudome, & Horikoshi, 2010; Gude, 2014; Liu et al., 2014; Qu, Wang, Ren, & Qu, 2013). Taking the carbon dots (CDs) as an example, they are a new class of ultra-small fluorescent nanoparticles with excellent photo-stability, environmental and biological compatibility, as well as great potential for applications in biomedical engineering fields (Durán, Simões, De Moraes, Fávaro, & Seabra, 2016). Generally speaking, the CDs are prepared by artificially methods such as hydrothermal reaction and microwave assisted synthesis under harsh conditions (Zuo, Lu, Sun, Guo, & He, 2016). Most recently, CDs produced during food cooking (e.g., roasting, baking etc.) have attracted our attention because of their peculiar properties (Liao et al., 2015; Sk, Jaiswal, Paul, Ghosh, & Chattopadhyay, 2012; Wang et al., 2015). During food thermal processing, food components are subjected to heat treatment leading to formation of carbonaceous nanoparticles (Cao et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2014; Li, Wang, Cong, & Tan, 2017). These CDs with large number of functional groups on their surfaces exhibit unique physicochemical properties, such as highly water solubility, strong fluorescence and good photostability. However, free radical scavenging capability of these foodborne CDs has been barely studied to date even though anti-oxidative stress is of importance for bio-system (Ju, Lee, Lee, Park, & Lee, 2011; Zhao et al., 2015). The evaluation of radical scavenging activity is critical in understanding their bio-effects. Highly reactive free radicals can cause biomolecule distortions, protein damage, lipid oxidation and cell apoptosis and clearing excess free radicals is essential for normal physiological processes (Li et al., 2017). These results revealed that more attention has to be paid to the free-radical scavenging ability of foodborne CDs produced during food processing to understand their anti-oxidative stress property.

Mackerel, one of the important commercial fish species available worldwide, refers to a number of different species of pelagic fish (Shui, Han, Gao, Miao, & Yanagimoto, 2009). Roasting and frying are common cooking methods for mackerel. The information about the formation of CDs in roasted mackerel is rare even though the presence of fluorescence nanoparticles in roasted protein foods has been reported in roasted pike eel (Bi et al., 2017), turbot (Bi et al., 2017), baked lamb (Wang & Xie et al., 2017) and grilled hamburger (Li et al., 2017) recently. At high temperature of roasting, the non-enzymatic-browning Maillard reaction could be one of the main routes for generation of the foodborne CDs in foods (Sk et al., 2012; Wei et al., 2014). The physicochemical property of these endogenous CDs from roasted fish is an interesting topic because of potential effect after uptake. The artificially prepared CDs are known to possess free radical scavenging capability (Wu, Weng, Tang, & Guo, 2016), however, no information concerning the anti-oxidative stress is reported for the endogenous CDs generated in popular roasted mackerel.

The objective in this article is to investigate the formation of foodborne fluorescent CDs generated during the roasting of mackerel and their scavenging capability to radicals (·OH and ·CH3), thus providing insight of the physicochemical properties for the endogenous nanoparticles. The mackerel CDs with strong fluorescence (quantum yield is ca. 12%) were extracted and purified in the mackerel roasted at 230 °C for 40 min. Their morphology and size were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Their elemental composition and functional groups were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Their fluorescence properties (emission and lifetime) were investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy. The resulted mackerel CDs exhibited effective anti-oxidative stress property and their abilities for free radicals scavenging were assessed against hydroxyl radicals and free radicals produced from methylene blue (MB)/visible-light photosensitization system. The novelty of the study is that, for the first time, the presence of CDs was found in the roasted mackerel, and the mackerel CDs showed unique radical scavenging capability. The mechanism of the radical scavenging or antioxidant activity in the CDs is possibly due to the exitance of functional moieties like hydroxyl groups on their surface. The results herein provided valuable insights about these fish-borne fluorescent nanoparticles, and their potential impact on human health (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CDs from the roasting mackerel
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh mackerel was immediately eviscerated and deboned, and the raw fish flesh was cut into 2 × 2 × 1 cm pieces and roasted in an electronic oven at 230 °C for 40 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the roasted flesh was soaked in 500 mL of ethanol for 2 h. The ethanol solution was then subject to rotary-evaporation to remove the solvent. The raw product was further extracted with dichloromethane to remove the hydrophobic phase containing fat or lipids, and the aqueous solution was dialyzed by a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off 3.5 kDa for 2 days. The dialysis solution was collected and freeze-dried in a vacuum oven. Finally, the CDs powder with a production yield of 0.05% (calculation based on the fresh flesh) was obtained for further analysis. The characterization of fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence lifetime and stability measurements can be seen in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of mackerel CD on hydroxyl radicals
ce:section.ce:para: 5 μL of DMPO (50 mmol L−1), 10 μL of EDTA-2Na–Fe2+ (0.6 mmol L−1), 8 μL of H2O2 and 39 μL CDs (140 mmol L−1) were added to 38 μL PBS (150 mmol L−1, pH 7.4), and the solution was stirred in a 40 °C water bath for 30 min. Sample was then put in a capillary (internal diameter: 0.5 mm; length: 100 mm) tube for measuring the hydroxyl radical in a Bruker BioSpin A200 ESR instrument. ESR spectra were recorded at center field of 3480 G, sweep width of 50 G, scan range of 50 G, receiver gain of 20, modulation amplitudes of 1 G and modulation frequency of 100-kHz.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the mackerel CD solution (1 mg/mL) in the presence of H2O2 (140 mmol L−1) and hydroxyl radicals from Fenton reaction were measured in the range of 220–500 nm. Fluorescence spectra of the mackerel CD solution (1 mg/mL) in the presence of H2O2 and hydroxyl radicals from Fenton reaction were also measured to investigate the fluorescence quenching effect by hydroxyl radicals. ESR spectra of hydroxyl radicals in the presence of mackerel CD solution at 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 mg/mL were recorded to study the scavenging capability of the CDs. The inhibition of hydroxyl radicals by the mackerel CDs was calculated as follows:
Inhibition%=(1-IC/I0)%. (1)
Where IC is the signal intensity of hydroxyl radicals with CDs at various concentration levels, and I0 is signal intensity of hydroxyl radicals without CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of mackerel CD on MB degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The absorption spectra of MB aliquots (20 μM) were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at different time intervals in the absence and presence of visible light (350–780 nm) to assess the mackerel CD (0.5 mg/mL) effects on MB degradation using MB solution as a blank control. The mixed MB and CD solution was stirred for 2 h for adsorption-desorption equilibrium before UV–vis absorption measurements. The concentration of the MB solution can be calculated from the absorption value obtained in the UV–vis measurements. The kinetic rate constant of MB degradation was calculated using Langmuir-Hinshelwood model as follows (Wang, Li, Zhuang, & Zhang, 2013):
(2)
In
(
C
0
C
)
=
K
a p p
× t
Where K app is apparent kinetic rate constant, C0 is initial concentration of MB and C is the MB concentration at irradiance time t.

Fluorescence spectra of CD aqueous solution in the presence of MB (20 μM) were recorded at different irradiation time to investigate the influence of free radicals produced from MB to the fluorescence of mackerel CDs.

The CD effects on free radicals from MB was further investigated using ESR method by irradiation MB solution (20 μM) at 0, 2, 4, and 6 min, respectively, in the absence and presence of mackerel CD solution (0.5 mg/mL). The ESR spectra of CD and DMPO-only or DMPO-only were measured as well for comparison.

Materials, instrumentation, fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime (τ) measurements, photo-stability experiment of the mackerel CDs, metal ions and pH efforts on fluorescent intensity of CDs please see supplementary material.

The curves and diagrams were plotted using Origin 2015 software (OriginLab, Corporation, MA, USA) and one-factor analysis of variance was performed using Tukey test for each of the parameters to test whether there were significant differences using commercial SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fluorescent CDs in roasted mackerel
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of fluorescent CDs in roasted mackerel has been rarely studied and little is known about the thermal-induced CDs during roasting in mackerel. The roasting process possibly forms harsh condition similar to that intensively reported hydrothermal methods for the artificially synthesized fluorescent CDs. The water-soluble CDs were extracted and purified from the roasted mackerel as displayed in Fig. 1 a with a production yield of 0.05% based on the calculation of fresh flesh weight. The ethanol extractant from raw flesh of mackerel dose not fluorescence under UV light irradiation (λex = 365 nm) (Fig. 1b), while that from roasted mackerel flesh emits a strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 1c). The strong fluorescence of the extraction of roasted flesh indicated that there was processing-induced fluorescent substance produced in the roasted flesh. The fluorescent substance from roasted mackerel was further confirmed by TEM. As Fig. 1d shows, the CDs purified from roasted mackerel were spherical and mono-dispersed nanoparticles. Their average size is about 2.2 nm with a relatively narrow size distribution in the range of 0.9–3.5 nm. The ultrasmall size of the nanoparticles from roasted mackerel is quite similar to those works reported previously (Li, Jiang, Wang, Cong, & Tan, 2017; Cao et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Bi et al., 2017). These fluorescent nanoparticles were possibly produced after complex reaction sequence involving dehydration, oxidation, polymerization, pyrolysis and carbonation of organic matters such as protein, lipid and polysaccharides at high temperature. This result further confirmed that CDs can be formed in foods during daily cooking process, with similar morphology and fluorescent properties as those CDs prepared by hydrothermal or microwave-assistant synthesis methods. Furthermore, these CDs were amorphous with no ordered lattice observable even in high resolution TEM images. Therefore, they probably belong to the family of polymer-like CDs, similar to those reported in the literature obtained with designed artificial synthesis methods (Song et al., 2015; Song, Zhu, Shao, & Yang, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and its mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, a maximum emission of 451 nm was found when they were excited at 380 nm, and the fluorescent intensity continually decreased as the excitation shifted to the longer wavelength. The fluorescent CDs extracted from roasted mackerel are strongly fluorescent with excitation-depended emission property similar to the artificially synthesized CDs (Zuo et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the maximum emission peak shifted from 425 nm to 480 nm as excitation wavelengths increased (Fig. 2b). This behavior is believed to be caused by the surface state that affects the band gap of the nanoparticles (Hill et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2012; Tan, Zhang, Li, Zhang, & Yue, 2016). The fluorescent decay curve in Fig. 2c demonstrates that the fluorescence lifetime was about 9.1 ns, which was comparable to the previous studies (Wang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Bi et al., 2017). The fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs at 380 nm was determined to be 12%, using quinine sulfate as standard, which was close to those reported for CDs from other food products (Bi et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017). Fig. 2d shows two absorption peaks at 278 and 329 nm in the UV–vis spectra, which are assigned to the π-π* and n-π* charge-transfer of CDs, respectively. The UV–vis spectrum of these fluorescent CDs extracted from roasted mackerel exhibits very similar profile as those from the pike eel and beef (Li et al., 2017; Bi et al., 2017), suggesting that the CDs originated from roasted protein foods have similar absorbance behavior.

The surface groups of the mackerel CDs are of importance in determining their properties. FTIR technique was used to examine the surface functional groups of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the FTIR spectrum of the roasted mackerel CDs had some similarity with that of mackerel fish powder. For instance, the main absorption peaks were observed at (3124–3400) cm−1 and (1534–1671) cm−1, which are assigned to O–H and CO stretching vibration, respectively. However, a closer inspection reveals that the O–H and CO peaks of the CDs are much stronger than those of the raw fish powder, indicating that more O–H and CO groups are produced during roasting. The absorption peaks at 2868-3031 cm−1, 1380-1468 cm−1 and 1000-1171 cm−1 were assigned to C–H2, C–H3, CC and C–O stretching vibration, respectively. The shapes of C–H2, C–H3, CC and C–O peaks of CDs were also different from those of fish powder. These results suggest that significant changes occurred to the surface functional groups between the CDs and the fish powder.

The elemental composition of the CDs was explored by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3b, the XPS spectrum indicates that the mackerel CDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements on their surfaces. The elemental composition of the CDs was calculated to be C 52.7%, N 24.1%, and O 22.3%, respectively, confirming carbon-rich nature in the CDs. The nitrogen content of 24.1% suggests that these CDs originated from fish protein. High-resolution C1s peak (Fig. 3c) was fitted into three peaks at 282.6, 284.1, 285.8 eV, corresponding to O–CO, C–O, and C–N groups, respectively. High-resolution N1s spectrum shows that the major peak at 397.5 eV reveals the presence of CN or C–N groups (Fig. 3d). These data show that the CDs contain abundant hydroxyl, carbonyl/carboxylate and nitrogen-containing groups on their surfaces. The result is consistent with the FTIR analysis.

Fluorescence stability of CDs was further investigated in a variety of environmental conditions. Firstly, fluorescence intensity of the CDs was measured under a continuous UV light irradiation. No obvious change was observed for CDs during 60 min irradiation (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the fluorophores of the CDs were very stable under UV light. Fig. 4b shows that Fe3+ had significant fluorescence quenching effect on the CDs among the tested metal ions and Cu2+ and Ni2+ quenching effects were relatively low. This can be attributed to nonradiative electron-transfer from the CDs’ excited state to the d-orbital of Fe3+ (Zhang et al., 2013). In some cases, metal ion strength may affect the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophores (Bi et al., 2017). Herein, the fluorescent intensity of the CDs in sodium chloride solutions with various concentrations was recorded. The result shown in Fig. 4c indicates that sodium chloride concentration had no effect on fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Furthermore, fluorescence-stability of the CDs was measured under various pH (2–11), the fluorescence intensity was stable for pH above 3 (Fig. 4d). In summary, the CDs exhibited good fluorescence stability, and this probably means that they were stable at various physiological environments.

All of the above results confirmed the presence of CDs in roasted mackerel. These CDs probably originated from the biopolymers like protein and lipid that were degraded into small molecules at high temperature, and then changed into polymer dots (Hu, Yang, & Yu, 2015). With the increase of surface temperature of flesh, the polymer dots became smaller and formed ordered nanostructure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of mackerel CD on hydroxyl radicals
ce:section.ce:para: Multifunctional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the mackerel CDs were found as confirmed in Figs. 2d and 3a. These functional groups may have the capability to scavenge free radicals. Hydroxyl radicals (·OH) from classical Fenton reaction were utilized to test the mackerel CDs anti-oxidative stress ability. In Fenton reaction, H2O2 and ferrous salts react to form hydroxyl radicals (Barati, Shamsipur, & Abdollahi, 2015). Since the mackerel CDs may interact with H2O2 and cause the fluorescence intensity to change, the effect of H2O2 on CDs was evaluated to go along with that of the hydroxyl radicals. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the absorption peaks at 278 and 329 nm disappeared after the addition of H2O2 to CD solution, and the free radical from Fenton reaction also resulted in the weakened absorption of the CDs. This finding suggested that the mackerel CDs was changing after interaction with the oxidant H2O2 and ·OH. H2O2 can oxidize the structure (carbonyl and carbon-carbon double bond) of CDs on the surface, which is similar to previous reports about H2O2 oxidizing conjugated polymer causing degradation (Liu, Wang, Cai, Zhou, & Liu, 2018). The fluorescence quenching results displayed in Fig. 5b further confirmed that the presence of ·OH resulted in much stronger fluorescence quenching compared with that of H2O2 alone. In addition, significant blue-shifting was observed when the mackerel CDs were treated with H2O2 and free radicals from Fenton reaction, as reported previously (Barati et al., 2015). The interaction with ·OH might lead to energy level changes in the CD structures. ESR spectra (Fig. 5c) show that the measured signal intensity of ·OH decreased with the increase of concentration of CDs. When the concentration of CDs reached 15 mg/mL, the signal of ·OH almost disappeared. A 90% decline in free radical signal was observed when the mackerel CDs were applied at the concentration of 15 mg/mL (Fig. 5d), indicating a strong dose-dependent scavenging capability of the CDs for hydroxyl radicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of mackerel CDs on methyl radical scavenging
ce:section.ce:para: Photo-induced radicals react with a wide range of biological molecules including DNA, cholesterol, lipids, amino acids, and proteins, and can cause damages to the organism (Rahmanto, Morgan, Hawkins, & Davies, 2010). In this study, the scavenging capability of the mackerel CDs for the free radicals produced from MB/visible-light photosensitization system was evaluated. The intense six-line hyperfine ESR spectra were recorded in the MB/visible-light photosensitization system (Fig. 6 a) using DMPO as the spin trapping agent, which was different from what has reported for singlet oxygen (Ye, Bruning, Yntema, Mayer, & Rijnaarts, 2017). The hyperfine splitting patterns were similar to that of the DMPO-CH3 complex with A N of 15.31G and A H of 22.0G (Penning, Ohnishi, Ohnishi, & Harvey, 1996). The formation of DMPO-CH3 is likely attributed to the DMPO reacting with methyl radical derived from MB/visible-light system. Strong ESR signals were observed along with increase of irradiation time in the absence of CDs (Fig. 6a), while very weak signal was observed at the same irradiation time duration in the presence of CDs (Fig. 6b). The presence of CDs strongly inhibited the formation of DMPO-CH3 complex, suggesting that the CDs also have scavenging capability for the free radicals produced from visible-light photosensitization system. In addition, almost no ESR signal was observed when the CD/DMPO or DMPO solution was illustrated for 20 min (Fig. 6c and d), revealing that the CDs themselves did not produce free radicals under the illumination of visible light.

Fig. 7 a shows that, under dark condition, there was hardly any difference of the residual ratio (C/C0) of MB in the absence and presence of CDs. However, significant increase of residual ratio of MB was observed under visible light irradiation upon the addition of mackerel CDs (Fig. 7b). This revealed that the CDs were able to scavenge the free radicals produced from MB/visible-light photosensitization and protect MB from degradation. The calculated reaction rate constant, Kapp, was 0.0158 for MB solution, and 0.0092 for MB solution in the presence of CDs (Fig. 7c). The value of Kapp decreased 41.4%, which indicated a significant drop in MB degradation rate due to the reduction of free radicals (being scavenged by CDs). In addition, the analysis of fluorescence spectral change of MB-containing CD aqueous solution indicated that the maximum emission peak blue-shifted as the irradiation time went on. Significant decrease of fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 7d). The emission peak was from mackerel CDs rather than MB. This result is consistent with previous studies about singlet oxygen causing fluorescence quenching of conjugated polymers (Altınok, Friedle, & Iii, 2013). It was also similar to those of CDs interacting with ·OH from Fenton reaction described above, suggesting that the energy level of CDs changed after they scavenged free radicals from MB/visible-light system. All the evidences suggest that the mackerel CDs have scavenging capability for free radicals.

In conclusion, the formation and physicochemical properties of fluorescent CDs with an average size of 2.2 nm in roasting mackerel were characterized. The ultra-small CDs mainly contain C 52.7%, N 24.1% and O 22.3% that have an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior. The abundant function groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface make them resistant to free radicals. The mackerel CDs resulted in a 90% decline for hydroxyl radicals when the concentration of CDs was 15 mg/mL. In addition, the reaction rate constant decreased 41.4%, demonstrating a significant drop in MB degradation rate because of the methyl radical scavenging property by CDs. Overall, the study provides insights to further explore foodborne CDs for their free radical scavenging capabilities, and the potential impact on human health.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Project (2017YFD0400103) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915).
